body.text: **Top story:** The Intercept and EFF expose critical gaps in OpenAI's Pentagon deal safeguards — the company's protections against surveillance and autonomous weapons rely on trust, not enforcement — while Florida's AI Bill of Rights stalls in the House despite a 35-2 Senate majority, Apple's $599 MacBook Neo generates mass-market AI buzz, and DeepSeek V4 enters Day 9 with no launch.

items.text: **🔍 1. OpenAI's Pentagon Deal: "You'll Just Have to Trust Us"**

body.text:
The Intercept published "OpenAI on Surveillance and Autonomous Killings: You're Going to Have to Trust Us" (March 8) — the most comprehensive critical examination yet of whether OpenAI's amended Pentagon deal actually prevents mass surveillance and autonomous weapons targeting.

**The core finding:** OpenAI's safeguards rely on trust and contractual promises — not technical enforcement mechanisms. The published contract excerpt "does not give OpenAI an Anthropic-style, free-standing right to prohibit otherwise-lawful government use."

**EFF analysis** ("Weasel Words"): The amended language is still ambiguous — Pentagon agreed to adhere to red lines only "as appropriate." Secret agreements have never been sufficient to rein in surveillance agencies. No substitute for enforceable legal limits.

**OpenAI's defense** ("Our agreement with the Department of War" blog post): NSA can't use tools without a separate agreement; deployment architecture will verify red lines. Sam Altman acknowledged the original deal "looked opportunistic and sloppy" (March 3), prompting amendments.

**Why it matters:** Anthropic drew hard lines and paid with a Pentagon blacklist. OpenAI said trust us. The Intercept and EFF say that's insufficient. This question now shapes the entire industry's regulatory trajectory.

items.text: **🏛️ 2. Florida AI Bill of Rights: Senate Passes 35-2, House Blocks It**

body.text:
DeSantis's "AI Bill of Rights" (SB 482) passed the Florida Senate 35-2 but was blocked by the House Speaker.

**What the bill does:** Gives parents the right to control children's interactions with AI chatbots; creates transparency requirements for AI in consequential decisions.

**Why the House blocked it:** Speaker Daniel Perez says AI regulation should be handled at the federal level, not state-by-state. A separate data center regulation bill is advancing in the House instead.

**Why it matters:** The 35-2 Senate vote signals overwhelming political appetite for AI guardrails — but the House position tracks with the current federal administration's deregulatory stance. If Florida can't pass AI regulation with a 35-2 majority, that signals significant structural resistance to state-level AI law nationwide.

items.text: **💻 3. Apple MacBook Neo: Mass-Market AI Computing at $599**

body.text:
Apple's MacBook Neo (announced March 4, ships March 11) dominates tech coverage as the most significant consumer AI hardware of the year:

• First Mac to use an A-series chip (A18 Pro) rather than M-series — same chip family as iPhone
• Starting price: **$599** (cheapest Mac ever, $400–$800 below MacBook Air/Pro)
• 3x faster on-device AI workloads vs. comparable Intel Core Ultra 5 PC
• Colors: blush, indigo, silver, citrus; 13-inch Liquid Retina display

Apple is explicitly positioning this as a mass-market **AI device**, not just a budget laptop. Puts structural pressure on Chromebooks and Windows budget laptops in the $400–$700 range — where AI PC marketing is most contested.

items.text: **⏳ 4. DeepSeek V4: Day 9 — Still No Launch**

body.text:
DeepSeek V4 enters Day 9 without an official announcement. Every anticipated release window since mid-February has been missed: Lunar New Year, late February, early March, and the "nuclear moment" community prediction for March 6-7.

**Current signals:** Chinese tech media spotted a possible "V4 Lite" update on DeepSeek's website with expanded context handling — but DeepSeek has not confirmed model name, specifications, or V4 timeline.

**Expected specs:** Trillion-parameter MoE model, ~32B active parameters, native multimodal, 1M-token context, Huawei Ascend optimization. Unverified benchmarks: 90% HumanEval (vs. Claude 88%, GPT-5.4 82%).

Every passing day solidifies GPT-5.4's benchmark narrative and gives OpenAI more time to entrench its post-boycott position. When V4 does arrive, expectations will be extremely elevated.

March 7's peak (2.5M boycotters, Claude at #1 App Store) showing early normalization on March 8:

• Claude App Store ranking holding in top 5 but no longer #1
• ChatGPT uninstall rate normalizing from the 295% day-over-day spike
• "Protest downloads" typically normalize within 3–5 days

Key question: how many users who switched to Claude actually stay? Even 10% conversion = 250,000 sustained new users. OpenAI's handling of the Pentagon deal created a documented brand trust gap that didn't exist before Feb 27 — and that gap remains regardless of whether the boycott sustains.

• **Anthropic lawsuit preview** — Filing expected March 9; emergency TRO hearing targeting March 24; tech industry groups preparing amicus briefs; State and Treasury reviewing Claude usage
• **GPT-5.4 consolidating** — With DeepSeek V4 absent, GPT-5.4's benchmark leadership continues unchallenged; three-variant lineup with 1.05M-token context driving enterprise adoption
• **Oracle layoffs begin** — First wave of 20,000–30,000 cuts reportedly beginning this month to fund AI infrastructure
• **MWC 2026 aftermath** — AI glasses partnerships (Alibaba/Qwen, Samsung, MediaTek, iFlytek) generating supply chain and ecosystem discussions post-show

• **Anthropic lawsuit (March 9)** — TRO request determines whether the Pentagon designation is paused; sets precedent for government AI vendor relationships
• **DeepSeek V4** — An imminent launch could dramatically shift competitive dynamics; the longer the delay, the more entrenched GPT-5.4's position
• **MacBook Neo reviews (March 11)** — Early benchmarks will determine whether iPhone chip architecture validates Apple's mass-market AI device thesis
• **Florida AI Bill** — Whether the House eventually takes it up; signals whether any U.S. state can pass comprehensive AI regulation in the current political environment
• **#QuitGPT Week 2** — Whether Claude retains any meaningful portion of its new user base beyond the protest surge
